items.correct: Water vapour released from plant leaves to the atmosphere.
items.misconception: Water flowing through soil into rivers.
items.why: Students confuse transpiration with other water movements. It's specifically water LOSS THROUGH PLANTS - significant in forested areas, contributing substantially to atmospheric water vapour over land.

items.q: What process moves carbon from the atmosphere into plants?
items.why: Students confuse the directions. Photosynthesis takes CO2 OUT of the atmosphere (plants build sugars); respiration RELEASES CO2. Both run simultaneously in plants, but only photosynthesis fixes new carbon.

items.q: Why are top predators most affected by persistent pollutants like mercury?
items.correct: Biomagnification concentrates pollutants at each trophic level up the food chain.
items.misconception: Predators are exposed to more pollution directly.
items.why: Students focus on direct exposure. The mechanism is concentration through eating contaminated prey - small accumulations in lower levels multiply upward, so apex predators have the highest body burdens.

items.q: Water loss from plant leaves is called?
items.why: Correct answer: Transpiration. “Evaporation” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Roughly what percent of Earth's freshwater is locked in glaciers and ice caps?
items.why: Exact answer: 69 %. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
